column1: We propose the following definition: Peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM) is defined as a form of dilated cardiomyopathy that occurs towards the end of pregnancy or in the months following delivery

Peripartum cardiomyopathy (PPCM) is a distinct form of dilated cardiomyopathy that manifests during the late stages of pregnancy or within five months following delivery. According to the most recent international consensus
column2: specifically the 2018 European Society of Cardiology (ESC) guidelines
column3: PPCM is characterized by the onset of heart failure symptoms and a left ventricular ejection fraction (LVEF) typically less than 45% (Regitz2018 pages 34-34
column5: Sliwa2010a pages 1-2). The diagnosis of PPCM is a diagnosis of exclusion
column6: necessitating thorough clinical evaluation to rule out other potential causes of heart failure
column7: including underlying chronic heart failure with reduced ejection fraction (HFrEF) that predates pregnancy (Sliwa2010a pages 2-2

column1: The differentiation between de novo PPCM and underlying HFrEF is critical for both management and prognosis. De novo PPCM occurs in women who have no prior history of cardiovascular disease
column2: presenting specifically in the peripartum period
column3: whereas HFrEF would have been present before pregnancy (Regitz2018 pages 38-39
column4: Sliwa2010a pages 4-5). This distinction is essential as it influences treatment strategies and informs patients about the risks associated with future pregnancies. Women with a history of HFrEF should be counseled regarding the potential for deterioration during gestation and the implications for subsequent pregnancies
column5: even if they have recovered normal LV function (Regitz2018 pages 38-39).

column1: The ESC guidelines emphasize the importance of accurate diagnosis to ensure appropriate treatment strategies are employed for affected women (Regitz2018 pages 36-37). The guidelines recommend that clinicians maintain a high index of suspicion for PPCM in women presenting with heart failure symptoms during the peripartum period
column2: particularly in those without a prior history of heart disease. The diagnostic process should include a detailed history and physical examination
column4: and laboratory tests to exclude other causes of heart failure (Sliwa2010 pages 1-2).
